---
title: "Feature Engineering"
subtitle: "BSAD 8310: Business Forecasting --- Lecture 11"
author: "BSAD 8310: Business Forecasting"
institute: "University of Nebraska at Omaha"
date: "Spring 2026"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, emory-clean.scss]
    slide-number: true
    show-slide-number: all
    transition: fade
    transition-speed: fast
    incremental: false
    toc: false
    center: false
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    margin: 0.05
    logo: ""
    footer: "BSAD 8310 | University of Nebraska at Omaha | Spring 2026"
    embed-resources: true
bibliography: ../Bibliography_base.bib
csl: https://www.zotero.org/styles/chicago-author-date
---

## Lecture 11: Outline

1. Lag Features
2. Rolling Statistics
3. Calendar & Structural Features
4. Interaction & Ratio Features
5. Feature Selection
6. Pipeline Design
7. Application to Forecasting

---

## Motivation: The Feature Gap

::: {.keybox}
**Lecture 10 best result:** LSTM with 36 features achieved RMSE $\approx$ 1,920 --- down from XGBoost's 2,250 (26 features). The improvement came mostly from *better inputs*, not a fancier model.
:::

**What feature engineering adds:**

- **Lag features:** give the model explicit access to past values, guided by ACF and PACF
- **Rolling statistics:** capture local trends, volatility, and momentum across multiple time scales
- **Calendar effects:** encode seasonality without requiring seasonal differencing
- **Interaction / ratio features:** year-over-year and month-over-month change rates that tree models cannot discover on their own
- **Pipeline design:** prevent data leakage by fitting transformations inside each cross-validation fold

**Lecture goal:** Build `make_features_extended()` (36 features), select the best subset, and update the Lectures 01--11 leaderboard.

---

# Lag Features

Converting time-series forecasting into a supervised regression problem

---

## Lag Features: Section Overview

**Lag features** are the most direct way to give a tree-based or linear model access to the past. They convert a time-series forecasting problem into a standard supervised regression problem with $y_{t-k}$ as predictors.

**Design questions:**

- Which lags are worth including? (ACF and PACF guidance)
- How many lags before diminishing returns?
- How to avoid look-ahead leakage when computing lags?

**Key rule:** always use `shift(k)` with $k \geq 1$ so that at prediction time for period $t$, only values observed at $t-1, t-2, \ldots$ are used.

---

## Choosing Lags: ACF and PACF Guidance

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="52%"}

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 260 290" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="width:100%; max-height:270px; display:block; margin:0 auto; font-family:inherit;">

  <!-- ===== ACF panel (y=150 to y=270) ===== -->
  <!-- Axis -->
  <line x1="30" y1="150" x2="30" y2="270" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.8"/>
  <line x1="30" y1="270" x2="248" y2="270" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.8"/>
  <!-- Axis label -->
  <text x="6" y="215" font-size="8" fill="#525252" transform="rotate(-90,10,215)">ACF</text>

  <!-- Confidence band (dashed) at y=241 (approx 0.29 of 2.0 scale) -->
  <line x1="30" y1="241" x2="248" y2="241" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.6" stroke-dasharray="3,2"/>

  <!-- ACF bars (lags 1-12): heights scaled from data -->
  <!-- Lag 1: 1.36 → height=68 → bar from y=270 to y=202 -->
  <rect x="33"  y="202" width="12" height="68" fill="#E8F0FA" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <!-- Lag 2: 1.15 → height=57.5 -->
  <rect x="50"  y="213" width="12" height="57" fill="#E8F0FA" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <!-- Lag 3: 0.93 → height=46.5 -->
  <rect x="67"  y="224" width="12" height="46" fill="#E8F0FA" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <!-- Lag 4: 0.72 → height=36 -->
  <rect x="84"  y="234" width="12" height="36" fill="#E8F0FA" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <!-- Lag 5: 0.51 → height=25.5 -->
  <rect x="101" y="244" width="12" height="26" fill="#E8F0FA" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <!-- Lag 6: 0.35 → height=17.5 -->
  <rect x="118" y="252" width="12" height="18" fill="#E8F0FA" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <!-- Lag 7: 0.24 → height=12 -->
  <rect x="135" y="258" width="12" height="12" fill="#E8F0FA" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <!-- Lag 8: 0.16 → height=8 -->
  <rect x="152" y="262" width="12" height="8"  fill="#E8F0FA" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <!-- Lag 9: 0.13 → height=6.5 -->
  <rect x="169" y="263" width="12" height="7"  fill="#E8F0FA" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <!-- Lag 10: 0.11 → height=5.5 -->
  <rect x="186" y="264" width="12" height="6"  fill="#E8F0FA" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <!-- Lag 11: 0.10 → height=5 -->
  <rect x="203" y="265" width="12" height="5"  fill="#E8F0FA" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <!-- Lag 12: 1.20 → height=60 (seasonal spike) -->
  <rect x="220" y="210" width="12" height="60" fill="#E8F0FA" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <!-- Lag 12 annotation -->
  <text x="226" y="206" font-size="7" fill="#005CA9" text-anchor="middle" font-weight="600">seasonal</text>

  <!-- ===== PACF panel (y=10 to y=130) ===== -->
  <!-- Axis -->
  <line x1="30" y1="10"  x2="30" y2="130" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.8"/>
  <line x1="30" y1="130" x2="248" y2="130" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.8"/>
  <!-- Axis label -->
  <text x="6" y="75" font-size="8" fill="#525252" transform="rotate(-90,10,75)">PACF</text>

  <!-- Confidence band at y=115.5 (0.29/2.0 * 100 = 14.5 from bottom) -->
  <line x1="30" y1="115" x2="248" y2="115" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.6" stroke-dasharray="3,2"/>

  <!-- PACF bars scaled: max height=80, baseline y=130 -->
  <!-- Lag 1: 1.36 → height=68 (unoblue, significant) -->
  <rect x="33"  y="62"  width="12" height="68" fill="#005CA9" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <!-- Lag 2: 0.29 → height=14.5 (light, borderline) -->
  <rect x="50"  y="115" width="12" height="15" fill="#E8F0FA" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <!-- Lags 3-11: small (gray, below band) -->
  <rect x="67"  y="125" width="12" height="5"  fill="#d0d0d0"/>
  <rect x="84"  y="125" width="12" height="5"  fill="#d0d0d0"/>
  <rect x="101" y="126" width="12" height="4"  fill="#d0d0d0"/>
  <rect x="118" y="126" width="12" height="4"  fill="#d0d0d0"/>
  <rect x="135" y="126" width="12" height="4"  fill="#d0d0d0"/>
  <rect x="152" y="127" width="12" height="3"  fill="#d0d0d0"/>
  <rect x="169" y="127" width="12" height="3"  fill="#d0d0d0"/>
  <rect x="186" y="127" width="12" height="3"  fill="#d0d0d0"/>
  <rect x="203" y="127" width="12" height="3"  fill="#d0d0d0"/>
  <!-- Lag 12: 1.12 → height=56 (unogreen, seasonal) -->
  <rect x="220" y="74"  width="12" height="56" fill="#F0FAF4" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="0.8"/>

  <!-- Lag 1 PACF annotation -->
  <text x="39" y="58" font-size="7" fill="#005CA9" text-anchor="middle" font-weight="600">AR(1)</text>
  <!-- Lag 12 PACF annotation -->
  <text x="226" y="70" font-size="7" fill="#15803d" text-anchor="middle" font-weight="600">s12</text>

  <!-- x-axis labels (shared for both panels, below ACF panel) -->
  <text x="39"  y="284" font-size="7" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">1</text>
  <text x="56"  y="284" font-size="7" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">2</text>
  <text x="73"  y="284" font-size="7" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">3</text>
  <text x="90"  y="284" font-size="7" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">4</text>
  <text x="107" y="284" font-size="7" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">5</text>
  <text x="124" y="284" font-size="7" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">6</text>
  <text x="141" y="284" font-size="7" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">7</text>
  <text x="158" y="284" font-size="7" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">8</text>
  <text x="175" y="284" font-size="7" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">9</text>
  <text x="192" y="284" font-size="7" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">10</text>
  <text x="209" y="284" font-size="7" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">11</text>
  <text x="226" y="284" font-size="7" fill="#15803d" text-anchor="middle" font-weight="600">12</text>
  <text x="139" y="292" font-size="7" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">Lag k</text>
</svg>
```

:::

::: {.column width="44%"}

**Reading the charts:**

- **ACF:** slow decay → persistent trend; spike at lag 12 → seasonal pattern
- **PACF:** significant at lag 1 only → pure AR(1); significant at lag 12 → include `lag_12`

::: {.definitionbox}
::: {.definitionbox-title}
Lag Selection Rule
:::
Include `lag_k` if the PACF at lag $k$ exceeds $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{n}$ (dashed band). For monthly RSXFS: include lags 1, 2, 12. Add lags 3--6 for robustness.
:::

[*Socratic: ACF at lag 12 is strong, but PACF at lag 12 is also strong. Why include `lag_12` even in an ARIMA model that handles seasonality through differencing?*]{.neutral .smaller}

:::

::::

---

## Lag Leakage: Safe Shift vs. Lookahead Bug

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="48%"}

**Safe: always `shift(k)`**

```python
import pandas as pd

# SAFE: shift(k) looks back k steps
# At time t we only see y[t-k]
df['lag_1']  = df['y'].shift(1)
df['lag_2']  = df['y'].shift(2)
df['lag_12'] = df['y'].shift(12)

# Rolling mean: shift FIRST
df['roll_mean_3'] = (
    df['y'].shift(1)
    .rolling(3).mean())

# No future information used.
print("Lag features are safe.")
```

:::

::: {.column width="48%"}

**Bug: missing `shift(1)`**

```python
# BUG: rolling without shift(1)
# includes y[t] = current target!
df['roll_mean_3'] = (
    df['y'].rolling(3).mean())

# At t=5, rolling uses:
#   y[3], y[4], y[5]  <- leakage!
# y[5] is what we are predicting.

# This inflates in-sample R^2
# but collapses on test data.

# Rule: always shift before
# any window operation.
```

::: {.warningbox}
Rolling without `shift(1)` is the #1 feature engineering bug. It causes dramatic overfitting that only appears on the test set.
:::

:::

::::

---

# Rolling Statistics

Local trends, volatility, and momentum over time

---

## Rolling Statistics: Section Overview

**Rolling statistics** capture local trends, volatility, and momentum over multiple time windows. They are especially powerful for tree-based models, which cannot express the concept of a "moving average" from raw lag features alone.

**Feature families:**

- **Rolling mean** ($w = 3, 6, 12$): local trend at multiple scales
- **Rolling std** ($w = 3, 6, 12$): local volatility --- useful for identifying calm vs. volatile regimes
- **Rolling min/max** ($w = 3, 6, 12$): range and extremes
- **Exponentially weighted mean (EWM):** gives more weight to recent observations than a simple average

All rolling operations must be computed on `df['value'].shift(1)` to prevent leakage (see the Lag Leakage slide).

---

## Rolling Statistics and EWM

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="52%"}

**Rolling window operations:**

::: {.smaller}

| **Feature** | **Formula** | **Captures** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| `roll_mean_w` | $\bar{y}_{t-w:t-1}$ | Local trend |
| `roll_std_w` | $s_{t-w:t-1}$ | Volatility |
| `roll_min_w` | $\min(y_{t-w},\ldots,y_{t-1})$ | Trough |
| `roll_max_w` | $\max(y_{t-w},\ldots,y_{t-1})$ | Peak |

:::

**Exponentially weighted mean:**

$$\mathrm{EWM}_t = \alpha\, y_{t-1} + (1-\alpha)\,\mathrm{EWM}_{t-1}, \quad \alpha \in (0,1)$$

With $\alpha = 0.3$: recent values receive ${\approx}3\times$ the weight of values 4 months back.

[Here $\alpha$ is the EWM decay weight --- distinct from the level-smoothing $\alpha$ in Lecture 03 (ETS) and the L1/L2 mixing $\alpha$ in Lecture 08 (Elastic Net).]{.neutral .smaller}

:::

::: {.column width="44%"}

::: {.definitionbox}
::: {.definitionbox-title}
Window Choice Heuristics
:::
- $w = 3$: captures quarter-long momentum
- $w = 6$: captures half-year cycles
- $w = 12$: captures full-year seasonality

Use all three; let the model select via regularization or feature importance.
:::

::: {.examplebox}
::: {.examplebox-title}
EWM vs. Rolling Mean
:::
EWM reacts faster to trend reversals. On RSXFS, EWM with $\alpha=0.3$ reduces lag behind turning points by $\sim$2 months compared with a 12-month rolling mean.
:::

:::

::::

---

## Which Features Matter? A Preview

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="54%"}

**Permutation importance (Random Forest, val set):**

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 300 220" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="width:100%; max-height:210px; display:block; margin:0 auto; font-family:inherit;">
  <!-- y-axis -->
  <line x1="90" y1="10" x2="90" y2="195" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.8"/>
  <!-- x-axis -->
  <line x1="90" y1="195" x2="285" y2="195" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="0.8" marker-end="url(#arr11imp)"/>
  <defs>
    <marker id="arr11imp" markerWidth="5" markerHeight="5" refX="4" refY="2.5" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L5,2.5 L0,5 Z" fill="#aaa"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
  <text x="287" y="199" font-size="7" fill="#525252">importance</text>

  <!-- Bars (top 4 unoblue, bottom 4 gray) -->
  <!-- lag_1: width=170 (most important) -->
  <rect x="90" y="15"  width="170" height="18" fill="#005CA9"/>
  <!-- lag_12: width=144 -->
  <rect x="90" y="40"  width="144" height="18" fill="#005CA9"/>
  <!-- lag_2: width=116 -->
  <rect x="90" y="65"  width="116" height="18" fill="#005CA9"/>
  <!-- roll_mean_12: width=96 -->
  <rect x="90" y="90"  width="96"  height="18" fill="#005CA9"/>
  <!-- roll_std_3: width=44 (gray) -->
  <rect x="90" y="115" width="44"  height="18" fill="#d0d0d0"/>
  <!-- month_Dec: width=36 -->
  <rect x="90" y="140" width="36"  height="18" fill="#d0d0d0"/>
  <!-- roll_mean_3: width=28 -->
  <rect x="90" y="155" width="28"  height="17" fill="#d0d0d0"/>
  <!-- month_Feb: width=20 -->
  <rect x="90" y="173" width="20"  height="17" fill="#d0d0d0"/>

  <!-- Feature labels -->
  <text x="86" y="28"  font-size="8" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="end" font-family="monospace">lag_1</text>
  <text x="86" y="53"  font-size="8" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="end" font-family="monospace">lag_12</text>
  <text x="86" y="78"  font-size="8" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="end" font-family="monospace">lag_2</text>
  <text x="86" y="103" font-size="8" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="end" font-family="monospace">roll_mean_12</text>
  <text x="86" y="128" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="end" font-family="monospace">roll_std_3</text>
  <text x="86" y="153" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="end" font-family="monospace">month_Dec</text>
  <text x="86" y="168" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="end" font-family="monospace">roll_mean_3</text>
  <text x="86" y="186" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="end" font-family="monospace">month_Feb</text>
</svg>
```

:::

::: {.column width="42%"}

**Key findings:**

- Lags 1 and 12 dominate --- confirming ACF/PACF
- `roll_mean_12` is the most important rolling feature
- `month_Dec` matters: December retail spike is real
- Calendar dummies are less important than rolling features when lags already capture seasonal levels

[This ranking is illustrative. Permutation importance is covered formally in Section 5 (Feature Selection).]{.neutral .smaller}

:::

::::

---

# Calendar & Structural Features

Encoding temporal position for tree-based models

---

## Calendar & Structural Features: Section Overview

**Calendar features** encode the position of a time step in the calendar --- month, quarter, year, holiday proximity. Unlike SARIMA's seasonal differencing, calendar dummies give tree-based models explicit information about which periods are structurally different.

**Key feature types:**

- **Month dummies:** 11 binary columns (`month_2` through `month_12`); January is the reference category
- **Quarter dummies:** 3 binary columns (Q2--Q4)
- **Trend term:** $t = 1, 2, \ldots, T$ for linear time trend
- **Structural break indicator:** 0/1 for pre/post a known break (recession, pandemic, policy change)

---

## Month Dummies: Construction and Interpretation

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="52%"}

**Construction in pandas:**

`df.index.month` $\rightarrow$ integers 1--12

`pd.get_dummies(df['month'], prefix='month', drop_first=True)`

**Interpretation (Random Forest):**

::: {.smaller}

| **Month** | **Avg. Effect** | **Business Meaning** |
|:---|---:|:---|
| January (ref) | --- | Post-holiday low |
| `month_11` | $+$1,800 | Nov. ramp-up |
| `month_12` | $+$3,200 | Holiday peak |
| `month_7` | $+$900 | Summer surge |
| `month_2` | $-$400 | Feb. dip |

:::

:::

::: {.column width="44%"}

::: {.examplebox}
::: {.examplebox-title}
Calendar vs. Seasonal Lag
:::
**Both encode seasonality, but differently:**

- `lag_12` $= y_{t-12}$: level effect --- captures the same magnitude as last year
- `month_12`: indicator effect --- always adds the same fixed amount for December, regardless of last year's level

Use both when trend is changing.
:::

[*Socratic: A SARIMA model with $s=12$ handles seasonality through seasonal differencing. Why might month dummies improve a Random Forest but add no value to SARIMA?*]{.neutral .smaller}

:::

::::

---

## When Calendar Features Help and Hurt

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="48%"}

**Calendar features help when:**

- The seasonal pattern is *fixed* across years (e.g., holiday retail, fiscal quarters)
- The model does not already capture seasonality via lag features (e.g., shallow trees, linear models without lag 12)
- Structural breaks are known and datable (add a 0/1 indicator for pre/post)

:::

::: {.column width="48%"}

**Calendar features can hurt when:**

- Seasonal patterns are *evolving* over time --- the fixed-effect assumption breaks down
- `lag_12` already accounts for seasonality --- month dummies add multicollinearity
- The dataset is small ($n < 100$): 11 month dummies consume degrees of freedom faster than they add signal

:::

::::

::: {.warningbox}
Never use calendar dummies as the sole source of seasonality in a neural network (LSTM). LSTMs learn temporal patterns from the sequence itself; adding 11 overlapping dummies adds noise. Use them as supplementary features only when lag features are already present.
:::

---

# Interaction & Ratio Features

Encoding change, not level

---

## Interaction & Ratio Features: Section Overview

**Interaction and ratio features** encode *change* rather than *level*. They help tree-based models detect acceleration or deceleration in a series --- a concept that requires computing ratios of two variables, something a single tree split cannot express directly.

**Key features:**

- **Year-over-year (YoY) change:** $\Delta^{(12)} y_t = y_{t-1} / y_{t-13} - 1$
- **Month-over-month (MoM) change:** $\Delta^{(1)} y_t = y_{t-1} / y_{t-2} - 1$
- **Lag interaction:** $y_{t-1} \times \mathbf{1}[\text{month} = 12]$ --- lets the model use different slopes for December

---

## Year-Over-Year and Month-Over-Month Formulas

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Year-over-year rate of change:**

$$\text{YoY}_t = \frac{y_{t-1}}{y_{t-13}} - 1$$

Note: both $y_{t-1}$ and $y_{t-13}$ are lagged to prevent leakage at prediction time for target $y_t$.

**Month-over-month rate of change:**

$$\text{MoM}_t = \frac{y_{t-1}}{y_{t-2}} - 1$$

**Why ratios instead of differences?** A 1,000-unit change means something different when the baseline is 10,000 vs. 100,000. Ratios are scale-free.

:::

::: {.column width="46%"}

::: {.definitionbox}
::: {.definitionbox-title}
Economic Interpretation
:::
For RSXFS retail sales:

- $\text{YoY}_t > 0$: economy is stronger than same month last year
- $\text{YoY}_t < 0$: contraction signal
- $|\text{MoM}_t|$ large: abnormal month-to-month swing (data revision or shock)

These features help the model distinguish a Christmas peak from an unexpected demand shock.
:::

[*Socratic: YoY removes the seasonal level but still contains trend. What additional transformation would make $\text{YoY}_t$ a stationary series?*]{.neutral .smaller}

:::

::::

---

## The Extended Feature Function (36 Features)

**Full feature engineering pipeline:**

```python
def make_features_extended(df,
                            lags=range(1, 13),
                            roll_windows=[3, 6, 12]):
    X = df[['value']].copy()
    # Lag features (ACF/PACF-guided, lags 1--12)
    for k in lags:
        X[f'lag_{k}'] = X['value'].shift(k)
    # Rolling stats (shift first to avoid leakage)
    for w in roll_windows:
        r = X['value'].shift(1).rolling(w)
        X[f'roll_mean_{w}'] = r.mean()
        X[f'roll_std_{w}']  = r.std()
        X[f'roll_min_{w}']  = r.min()
        X[f'roll_max_{w}']  = r.max()
    # Exponentially weighted mean
    X['ewm_alpha03'] = (
        X['value'].shift(1)
        .ewm(alpha=0.3, adjust=False).mean())
    # Calendar dummies (Jan = reference category)
    months = pd.Series(
        X.index.month, index=X.index)
    dums = pd.get_dummies(
        months, prefix='month',
        drop_first=True)
    X = pd.concat([X, dums], axis=1)
    # Return 36-column feature matrix
    return X.drop(columns='value').dropna()
```

---

# Feature Selection

Choosing the best subset of 36 candidate features

---

## Feature Selection: Section Overview

More features are not always better. With 36 features and $n \approx 300$ observations, we risk overfitting --- especially in linear models. **Feature selection** identifies the subset of features that maximizes out-of-sample predictive accuracy.

**Three strategies covered today:**

1. **LASSO** (from Lecture 08): $\ell_1$ penalty shrinks irrelevant feature coefficients to exactly zero. Implicit selection via the regularization path.
2. **Permutation importance:** measure how much test RMSE rises when a feature's values are randomly shuffled. Model-agnostic.
3. **RFECV:** Recursive Feature Elimination with cross-validation. Fit model, remove weakest feature, repeat until CV score stops improving.

---

## Feature Selection via LASSO

**LASSO regularization path** (Lecture 08 connection):

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}^{\text{LASSO}} = \operatorname{arg\,min}_{\boldsymbol{\beta}}
\underbrace{\sum_{t} (y_t - \mathbf{x}_t^\top \boldsymbol{\beta})^2}_{\text{prediction error}}
+ \lambda \underbrace{\|\boldsymbol{\beta}\|_1}_{\text{selection penalty}}$$

As $\lambda$ increases along the regularization path:

- **Lag 12 and lag 1** coefficients survive to large $\lambda$ --- high marginal importance
- **Rolling std features** shrink toward zero early --- lower marginal importance
- At CV-optimal $\lambda^*$: many coefficients are exactly zero (automatic feature selection) [@Tibshirani1996]

[LASSO requires standardized features --- all inputs should be on comparable scales (use `StandardScaler` inside a `Pipeline` to prevent leakage).]{.neutral .smaller}

---

## Feature Selection via Permutation Importance

::: {.definitionbox}
::: {.definitionbox-title}
Algorithm: Permutation Importance [@Molnar2022]
:::
::: {.smaller}
1. Fit Random Forest on training set
2. Record **baseline** validation RMSE: $\text{RMSE}_0$
3. For each feature $j$: shuffle column $j$, re-predict, record $\text{RMSE}_j$
4. $\text{Importance}_j = \text{RMSE}_j - \text{RMSE}_0$ (larger = more important)
:::
:::

**Advantages over split-count (impurity) importance:**

- Evaluated on *validation* set --- not biased toward high-cardinality features
- *Model-agnostic*: applies to LSTM, XGBoost, linear models
- Measures *marginal* contribution with all other features present

[On RSXFS: `lag_1`, `lag_12`, `roll_mean_12`, `ewm_alpha03` are the top four. See the importance preview in Section 2 (Rolling Statistics).]{.neutral .smaller}

---

## RFECV: Recursive Feature Elimination

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="49%"}

::: {.definitionbox}
::: {.definitionbox-title}
RFECV Algorithm
:::
::: {.smaller}
**Input:** estimator, feature matrix $X$, cross-validator

**Repeat:**

1. Fit estimator on all remaining features
2. Rank features by importance
3. Eliminate the lowest-ranked feature

**Select:** the subset size that maximizes mean CV score [@Guyon2003]
:::
:::

**Key parameters:**

- `step=1`: eliminate one feature per round
- `cv=TimeSeriesSplit(gap=1)`: prevents temporal leakage during selection
- `min_features_to_select=5`: floor to avoid trivial models

:::

::: {.column width="48%"}

```python
from sklearn.feature_selection import RFECV
from sklearn.ensemble import (
    RandomForestRegressor)
from sklearn.model_selection import (
    TimeSeriesSplit)

estimator = RandomForestRegressor(
    n_estimators=200, random_state=42)

tscv = TimeSeriesSplit(n_splits=5, gap=1)

selector = RFECV(
    estimator=estimator,
    step=1,
    cv=tscv,
    scoring='neg_mean_squared_error',
    min_features_to_select=5)

selector.fit(X_tr, y_tr)

# Selected feature names
selected = X_tr.columns[
    selector.support_].tolist()
print(f"Selected: {len(selected)} feats")
```

:::

::::

---

# Pipeline Design

Preventing leakage in cross-validation

---

## Pipeline Design: Section Overview

A **sklearn Pipeline** chains transformations and a model into a single object. Inside cross-validation, the pipeline re-fits every transformation on the training fold only --- preventing the subtle leakage that occurs when scaling with statistics from the full dataset.

**Why pipelines matter for time-series CV:**

- `StandardScaler` fitted on all data leaks test-set mean and variance into training
- With `Pipeline` + `TimeSeriesSplit`: scaler is fitted fresh on each train fold
- Same principle applies to `RFECV` and any imputer --- fit only on train, transform both train and test [@Pedregosa2011]

[Rule of thumb: if a transformation looks at the target variable (e.g., LabelEncoder, WoE encoding) or at the distribution of $X$ (e.g., StandardScaler, PCA), it *must* go inside the pipeline.]{.neutral .smaller}

---

## Pipeline Flow: From Features to Predictions

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="44%"}

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 200 330" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="width:100%; max-height:310px; display:block; margin:0 auto; font-family:inherit;">
  <defs>
    <marker id="arr11pipe" markerWidth="5" markerHeight="5" refX="4" refY="2.5" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L5,2.5 L0,5 Z" fill="#aaa"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>

  <!-- Stage 1: Raw Data -->
  <rect x="10" y="10"  width="180" height="38" rx="4" fill="#E8F0FA" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="100" y="27" font-size="9" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="middle" font-weight="600">Raw Data</text>
  <text x="100" y="40" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">(RSXFS)</text>
  <!-- Arrow 1→2 -->
  <line x1="100" y1="48" x2="100" y2="60" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1" marker-end="url(#arr11pipe)"/>

  <!-- Stage 2: Feature Engineering -->
  <rect x="10" y="60"  width="180" height="38" rx="4" fill="#F0FAF4" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="100" y="77" font-size="9" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="middle" font-weight="600">Feature Engineering</text>
  <text x="100" y="90" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">(36 features)</text>
  <!-- Arrow 2→3 -->
  <line x1="100" y1="98" x2="100" y2="110" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1" marker-end="url(#arr11pipe)"/>

  <!-- Stage 3: TimeSeriesSplit -->
  <rect x="10" y="110" width="180" height="38" rx="4" fill="#F0FAF4" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="100" y="127" font-size="9" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="middle" font-weight="600">TimeSeriesSplit</text>
  <text x="100" y="140" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">(gap=1)</text>
  <!-- Arrow 3→4 -->
  <line x1="100" y1="148" x2="100" y2="160" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1" marker-end="url(#arr11pipe)"/>

  <!-- Stage 4: StandardScaler -->
  <rect x="10" y="160" width="180" height="38" rx="4" fill="#F0FAF4" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="100" y="177" font-size="9" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="middle" font-weight="600">StandardScaler</text>
  <text x="100" y="190" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">(fit on train fold)</text>
  <!-- Arrow 4→5 -->
  <line x1="100" y1="198" x2="100" y2="210" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1" marker-end="url(#arr11pipe)"/>

  <!-- Stage 5: Model -->
  <rect x="10" y="210" width="180" height="38" rx="4" fill="#FDECEA" stroke="#E41C38" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="100" y="227" font-size="9" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="middle" font-weight="600">Model</text>
  <text x="100" y="240" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">(RF / XGBoost / LSTM)</text>
  <!-- Arrow 5→6 -->
  <line x1="100" y1="248" x2="100" y2="260" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1" marker-end="url(#arr11pipe)"/>

  <!-- Stage 6: Predictions -->
  <rect x="10" y="260" width="180" height="38" rx="4" fill="#E8F0FA" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="100" y="277" font-size="9" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="middle" font-weight="600">Predictions</text>
  <text x="100" y="290" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">ŷ_{T+h}</text>
</svg>
```

:::

::: {.column width="52%"}

**What happens inside each CV fold:**

::: {.smaller}
1. **Split:** `TimeSeriesSplit(gap=1)` --- 1-step gap prevents leakage
2. **Fit scaler:** `StandardScaler` on train fold *only*
3. **Transform:** same params applied to train and val
4. **Fit model:** on scaled train fold
5. **Score:** RMSE on scaled val fold
6. **Report:** average across all folds
:::

::: {.examplebox}
::: {.examplebox-title}
The Leakage Trap
:::
Fitting `StandardScaler` before CV on all data: validation mean/std seep into training. Typical RMSE improvement from fixing this: 50--200 units on RSXFS.
:::

:::

::::

---

## Building a Leakage-Free Pipeline

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Step 1: Construct the pipeline**

```python
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.preprocessing import (
    StandardScaler)
from sklearn.ensemble import (
    RandomForestRegressor)
from sklearn.model_selection import (
    TimeSeriesSplit, cross_val_score)
import numpy as np

# Build pipeline (order matters!)
pipe = Pipeline([
    ('scaler', StandardScaler()),
    ('model', RandomForestRegressor(
        n_estimators=300,
        random_state=42))])

tscv = TimeSeriesSplit(
    n_splits=5, gap=1)
```

:::

::: {.column width="47%"}

**Step 2: Cross-validate and predict**

```python
# CV: scaler fit on each train fold
scores = cross_val_score(
    pipe,
    X.values,
    y.values,
    cv=tscv,
    scoring=(
        'neg_mean_squared_error'))
rmse_cv = np.sqrt(-scores.mean())
print(f"CV RMSE: {rmse_cv:,.0f}")

# Refit on full training data
pipe.fit(X_tr.values, y_tr.values)

# Predict on held-out test set
y_pred = pipe.predict(
    X_te.values)
rmse_te = np.sqrt(
    np.mean((y_te - y_pred)**2))
print(f"Test RMSE: {rmse_te:,.0f}")
```

:::

::::

---

# Application to Forecasting

Baseline vs. extended features on RSXFS retail sales

---

## Application to Forecasting: Section Overview

Apply `make_features_extended()` (36 features) to RSXFS retail sales and compare against the baseline 26-feature set from Lectures 07--10. Measure the marginal contribution of better features holding the model class fixed.

**Evaluation design:**

- **Baseline** (26 features): 12 lags, 6 rolling means, 6 rolling stds, 2 ratios --- as in Lectures 07--10
- **Extended** (36 features): adds rolling min/max, EWM, and 11 month dummies
- **Models:** Elastic Net, Random Forest, XGBoost, LSTM (median over 5 seeds)
- **Holdout:** same chronological 15% test set used throughout the course

---

## Leaderboard: Baseline vs. Extended Features

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="56%"}

**Test-set RMSE on RSXFS:**

::: {.smaller}

| **Model** | **26f** | **36f** | $\Delta$RMSE |
|:---|---:|---:|---:|
| Seasonal Naive | 4,210 | --- | --- |
| SARIMA(1,1,1)(1,1,1)$_{12}$ | 2,840 | --- | --- |
| Elastic Net | 2,540 | 2,410 | $-$130 |
| Random Forest | 2,380 | 2,210 | $-$170 |
| XGBoost | 2,250 | 2,050 | $-$200 |
| LSTM (2-layer, $T\!=\!24$) | 2,180 | 1,920 | $-$260 |

:::

[Values are illustrative. LSTM = median over 5 random seeds.]{.neutral .smaller}

:::

::: {.column width="40%"}

**Interpretation:**

- All four ML models improve with richer features; LSTM gains most
- The improvement is larger for more flexible models --- extra features give trees more split choices
- Elastic Net gains least: its penalty already prevents overfitting to noisy features

::: {.keybox}
Feature engineering delivers larger improvements than switching model class. **RMSE: LSTM 36f $<$ XGBoost 36f $<$ LSTM 26f**: the feature gap dominates the model gap.
:::

:::

::::

---

# Takeaways and References

What we learned and where to go next

---

## Lecture 11 Key Takeaways

::: {.keybox}
::: {.smaller}
1. **Lag features** convert time series to supervised regression; use ACF and PACF to select which lags to include [@BoxJenkins2015].
2. **Rolling statistics** (mean, std, min, max, EWM) capture local trend and volatility at multiple scales. Always `shift(1)` before rolling to prevent leakage.
3. **Calendar dummies** help tree models detect recurring peaks and troughs; they add less value when `lag_12` is already present.
4. **Feature selection** (LASSO, permutation importance, RFECV) reduces overfitting and identifies the most informative signals [@Guyon2003; @Molnar2022].
5. **Pipelines** are essential for leakage-free cross-validation. Fit all transformations inside the CV loop, not on the full dataset [@Pedregosa2011].
6. **Feature engineering beats model selection:** on RSXFS, moving from 26 to 36 features reduced RMSE by 130--260 units across all model classes.
:::
:::

**Preview of Lecture 12:** Capstone and Applications --- combining the full Lectures 01--11 toolkit on a business case study with model selection, uncertainty quantification, and presentation-ready visualizations.

---

## References {.smaller}

::: {#refs}
:::
